text: Spintronics holds tremendous promise for future technologies such as quantum computing and memory. One major goal for the development of molecular materials for these applications is the synthesis of materials that can act as spin qubits at ambient conditions. Potential materials have been proposed using photoexcited chromophore–radical pairs, yet none have yet been extensively characterized. In the present study, we use transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) to examine photoexcited species formed in three different chromophore–radical pairs consisting of perylene diimide (PDI) as the chromophore paired with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) as the radical. Investigations of TEMPO isotopic labeling on the pairs lead to a quantitative determination of their molecular quartet compositions in their photoexcited states and to analyses of the electron spin coherence times, T m. Using these pairs in a rigid polymer matrix at ambient temperature enabled the attainment of the first demonstration of a modifiable spin material at room temperature with a value of 0.7 μs.
